beats.text: A language model and a robot policy are the same shape of object: both read a context and emit the next symbol.
The difference is the consequence. The model's mistake costs a token; the robot's mistake costs a dropped cup or a stripped gear.
Control is a partially observed Markov decision process: a policy maps observation history and a goal to a distribution over actions.

visual.note: swap next token for next action and a transformer is a policy

beats.text: The output is continuous: a seven degree-of-freedom arm command is seven real numbers, not a pick from a fixed vocabulary.
Errors compound, because each action changes the world the next observation is drawn from, drifting the robot into unseen states.
Real data is brutally expensive: a web crawl is free, but a demonstration is a human teleoperating a physical arm in real time.
A vision-language-action model is a vision-language model whose output space is extended to include motor commands.
RT-2 writes each action as a short string of tokens drawn from the model's existing vocabulary, then generates it like a sentence.
Tokenization clips a dimension to a range, splits it into B uniform bins, and maps a value to its bin index.

visual.note: RT-2 used B = 256 bins per dimension

beats.text: The round trip is lossy. Over a normalized range with 256 bins, the worst-case quantization error is about 0.0039.
π0 keeps the backbone but rejects bins, attaching an action expert that produces continuous chunks by flow matching.
Continuous heads buy smooth high-frequency control: π0 runs up to roughly fifty hertz and commits to coherent multi-step motion.
Simulation is the escape from data scarcity: a physics engine can generate millions of trajectories overnight with perfect labels.
The catch is the reality gap: contact dynamics, friction, sensor noise, latency, and lighting all differ from the real world.
Domain randomization widens the simulator's parameters so the real world looks like just another sample from training.

visual.note: maximize return averaged over a family of simulators

beats.text: If the parameter distribution is wide enough to contain reality, the gap collapses into ordinary in-distribution generalization.
Real return follows a U-shaped curve in randomization width: too narrow fails to transfer, too wide grows over-conservative.
System identification centers the distribution on truth, and a small batch of real episodes is often the highest-leverage hour.
Two recipes turn data into a policy, sitting at opposite ends of a sample-efficiency and safety trade-off.
Behavior cloning fits the policy by supervised learning, minimizing the gap between its action and the expert's.

visual.note: LLM pretraining with actions for tokens

beats.text: Cloning a one-D policy on four expert pairs gives a least-squares slope of about 0.77, recovered from demonstrations alone.
Its fatal flaw is covariate shift: trained only on expert states, the policy drifts into states where its errors are larger still.
Reinforcement learning lets the policy improve past the demonstrator, but can need orders of magnitude more interaction.
The pragmatic stack is imitation first, RL second: clone a competent base, then fine-tune for the last reliability.
Every modality unlocked once enough paired data existed. Text had the web, video had YouTube; robotics has no such corpus.
The internet records what the world looks like, never the torques and grasps that act on it.
Frontier language models train on the order of ten-to-the-thirteen tokens, a scale robotics cannot approach.

visual.note: all of shared robotics data is many orders smaller

beats.text: The largest open robot dataset pooled roughly one million trajectories across twenty-two embodiments.
A million trajectories of a hundred steps is ten-to-the-eight decision steps, still about a thousand times smaller than an LLM's set.
The honest summary: the architecture caught up to language models, but the data did not.
